---
title: "Figures"
---

Five publication-quality figures generated by `visualizer.py` in the
production pipeline. Each is committed to `figures/` in this site so the
page renders without requiring a re-run.

## Figure 1 — National time series

![National monthly typhoid case counts (2015–2023).](figures/fig1_time_series.png){#fig-timeseries}

Total monthly typhoid case counts aggregated across all 77 districts.
Seasonal peaks coincide with the monsoon (June–September) and the
post-monsoon contamination window. The trend reveals year-on-year
variability that motivates predictive modelling.

## Figure 2 — Correlation heatmap

![Feature correlation matrix.](figures/fig2_correlation_heatmap.png){#fig-corrheatmap}

Pearson correlations among the engineered feature set, including
log-transformed cases. Strong intra-climate correlations (e.g.
temperature and humidity) motivate the use of tree-based models, which
handle correlated features more gracefully than linear models.

## Figure 3 — Feature importance (XGBoost)

![XGBoost feature-importance ranking.](figures/fig3_feature_importance.png){#fig-importance}

Gain-based feature importance from the trained XGBoost model. Lagged
precipitation, lagged temperature, and the autoregressive `cases_lag1`
dominate the top of the ranking — consistent with the epidemiological
mechanism described in [Section 4.2](paper/04-results.qmd#sec-results).

## Figure 4 — Actual vs. Predicted

![Actual vs. predicted case counts across all four models.](figures/fig4_actual_vs_predicted.png){#fig-pred}

Test-set actuals overlaid with the four model predictions for the
held-out final 12 months. The Weighted Ensemble (dark line) tracks the
actuals most closely, especially during peak-season turning points.

## Figure 5 — Seasonal boxplot

![Monthly case-count distribution by calendar month.](figures/fig5_seasonal_boxplot.png){#fig-seasonal}

Boxplots of district-monthly case counts by calendar month, pooled
across 2015–2023. Median incidence during monsoon months (June–
September) is approximately 2–3× higher than non-monsoon months,
supporting the `monsoon` binary feature and motivating the climate-
driven prediction approach.
